g.data-narration: Welcome to our lecture on World Wide Web and HTTP - the foundation of modern web communication.
The World Wide Web is a global system of interconnected documents developed by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN in 1989.
The Web uses client-server architecture where browsers request resources and servers respond with content.
A URL uniquely identifies web resources with components: scheme, hostname, port, path, query string, and fragment.
HTTP is a stateless request-response protocol operating on TCP. The client initiates requests and the server responds.
HTTP defines methods like GET, POST, PUT, DELETE indicating the action. GET retrieves, POST submits, PUT replaces, DELETE removes.
g.text: Idempotent: GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS

g.data-narration: HTTP status codes indicate request results: 2xx success, 4xx client errors, 5xx server errors, 3xx redirects, 1xx informational.
g.text: 400 Bad Request | 401 Unauthorized | 403 Forbidden | 500 Internal Server Error

g.data-narration: An HTTP request consists of a request line, headers, and optional body. The request line contains method, path, and HTTP version.
An HTTP response contains status line with version, status code, message, followed by headers and response body.
HTTP headers provide metadata about requests and responses. Categories include general, request, and response headers.
Cookies maintain state in stateless HTTP. Server sets cookie via Set-Cookie header, browser sends it with subsequent requests.
g.text: Cookie Attributes: Expires | Path | Domain | Secure | HttpOnly
Session Cookie: Deleted when browser closes
Persistent Cookie: Active until expiration date

g.data-narration: HTTPS uses SSL/TLS encryption for secure communication, providing confidentiality, integrity, and authentication.
g.text: SSL/TLS: Encrypts data + Verifies server identity via certificates

g.data-narration: Key points: Web uses client-server model, HTTP is stateless, cookies enable sessions, HTTPS provides security.
Remember: GET in URL, POST in body, 404 is not found, 401 vs 403 auth, HTTP stateless, HTTPS port 443.
g.text: GET: URL params | POST: Body data
